teaching_card.title: Absolute-Value Equations (Easy)
teaching_card.body: |expression| = c means the expression equals c OR −c, giving two solutions. Solve both linear cases. The two solutions are symmetric around the value that makes the inside zero.
key_points: Split into two cases: = c and = −c.
|x − a| = c -> x = a + c or x = a − c.

screens.title: Two cases
screens.body: |expression| = c means the inside equals c OR equals −c, because both are distance c from zero. So an absolute-value equation usually has two solutions — solve both linear cases.
key_points: Expect two solutions, one on each side.

screens.title: With a coefficient
screens.body: If there's a coefficient, like |kx − b| = c, still split into the two cases (kx − b = c and kx − b = −c), then solve each for x by isolating and dividing by k.
key_points: Split first, then solve each case for x.
Divide by the coefficient at the end.

screens.title: Absolute-value inequalities
screens.body: |x − a| ≤ c means x is within c of a, i.e., a − c ≤ x ≤ a + c — a whole range. To count integers in an inclusive range, do high − low + 1 (here, 2c + 1).
key_points: |x − a| ≤ c -> a − c ≤ x ≤ a + c.
Integer count of an inclusive range = high − low + 1.

screens.title: Try the moves
screens.body: |x − 3| = 5 -> x − 3 = 5 or −5 -> x = 8 or −2. And |x − 3| ≤ 5 means −2 ≤ x ≤ 8, which contains 8 − (−2) + 1 = 11 integers.
